coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterial synthesis using a greener route has received massive attention as a sustainable, feasible, reliable, cost-effective, wealthy and environmentally friendly paradigm. Perhaps, synthesis via a greener route is considered as a sophisticated tool to minimize the toxic effects agglomerated with the conventional procedures of synthesis adapted for nanomaterials generally preferable in industry and laboratory. This work outlines the basic processes on the advanced patterns of the eco-friendly and various approaches involved in the green synthesis and its mechanism particularly accounted to metal and metal-based oxides like silver (Ag), gold (Au), zinc oxide (ZnO) and copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles with the aid of plant extracts. This review article encompasses the essential purpose of biological approaches, green roué for nanoparticle synthesis, the impact of various operating parameters, and solvent-based techniques. The toxicity/stability of nanomaterials and the occluded surface engineering protocols for deriving biocompatibility along with various solvent systems have also been outlined. Various mechanisms involved for green synthesis, merits of greener chemistry and its application in various fields are emphasized. Finally, the prospects of green route strategies for metal/metal oxide nanomaterials synthesis are proposed for further development. The challenges in utilizing the green synthesized metal/metal oxide nanoparticles are discussed. The recommendations of the green chemistry towards the cleaner production are emphasized.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterial synthesis using a greener route has received massive attention as a sustainable, feasible, reliable, cost-effective, wealthy and environmentally friendly paradigm. Perhaps, synthesis via a greener route is considered as a sophisticated tool to minimize the toxic effects agglomerated with the conventional procedures of synthesis adapted for nanomaterials generally preferable in industry and laboratory. This work outlines the basic processes on the advanced patterns of the eco-friendly and various approaches involved in the green synthesis and its mechanism particularly accounted to metal and metal-based oxides like silver (Ag), gold (Au), zinc oxide (ZnO) and copper oxide (CuO) nanoparticles with the aid of plant extracts. This review article encompasses the essential purpose of biological approaches, green roué for nanoparticle synthesis, the impact of various operating parameters, and solvent-based techniques. The toxicity/stability of nanomaterials and the occluded surface engineering protocols for deriving biocompatibility along with various solvent systems have also been outlined. Various mechanisms involved for green synthesis, merits of greener chemistry and its application in various fields are emphasized. Finally, the prospects of green route strategies for metal/metal oxide nanomaterials synthesis are proposed for further development. The challenges in utilizing the green synthesized metal/metal oxide nanoparticles are discussed. The recommendations of the green chemistry towards the cleaner production are emphasized.

Merits of greener chemistry based processes
Challenges in utilizing metal and metal oxide nanoparticles

ce:section.ce:para: Novel techniques for synthesizing nanomaterials such as metallic nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, quantum dots, silver, graphene, magnesium and their nano composites have gained recent attention in the field of nanotechnology (Rana et al., 2020). To synthesize nanomaterials of desirable shapes, size and specific functionalities, commonly two modes of fundamental approaches (top-down/bottom-up method) have been established in various works of literature (Singh et al., 2018). In the former method, various nanomaterials were synthesized using different approaches such as ball milling, lithographic method, sputtering technique and etching (Cao, 2004; Naghdi et al., 2017). The latter bottom-up method involves various techniques such as the sol-gel method, chemical vapor deposition, laser pyrolysis, spraying technique and molecular/atomic decomposition (Vidya et al., 2013; Ajeel et al., 2021a). Moreover, the morphological characteristics (size/structure/shape) of nanomaterials can be varied by modifying the composition/concentration of reactants and reaction conditions (pH, temperature, catalyst, feed material) (Hoffmann et al., 1995). These nanomaterials are put forth to various applications such as catalysts (Saif et al., 2016), adsorbents for pollutant removal (Ambika and Sundrarajan, 2015), heavy metal sensing (Singh et al., 2018), heat transfer agents (Ajeel et al., 2019a) etc., therein they can face certain limitations: 1. lack of deprived knowledge to understand the governing mechanism and other modeling parameters, 2. lack of continuous stability in the host environment, 3. analysis requirements, 4. bioaccumulation, 5. toxicity effects, 6. demand for skilled technicians, 7. drawbacks created during device designing and assembling structures, 8. reuse/recycle/regeneration strategy (Laurent et al., 2008; Jadoun et al., 2021). These challenges are rendering new and novel opportunities in such emerging and developing fields of the research world (Zangeneh et al., 2020). Nowadays, it is desired that the properties, occluded behavior, and other various classes of nanomaterials must be invented to focus on the above-mentioned points. (O'Neal et al., 2016).

To overcome the aforementioned challenges, the novel technique of green synthesis procedures is receiving more interest in the current era of research and the establishment of material technology (Mondal et al., 2020). Synthesis of nanomaterials through green routes governed by proper regulation, systematic control, clean-up activity, bioremediation process will abruptly aid to uplift their nature of environmental stability and suitability (Sastry et al., 2003; Khalaj et al., 2020). Few basic features of such green routes can be described using several factors such as minimization/prevention of waste, sequestration or removal of contaminants/pollutants, complete reduction of intermediates, use of suitable solvent and sustainable feedstock material (Narayanan et al., 2011; Jin et al., 2018).

Green synthesis is preferred as it minimizes the generation of useless or toxic by-products through the construction of sustainable, reliable and environmentally friendly procedures adapted for synthesis. The involvement of selective solvent groups and organic/natural resources is mandatory to secure this goal. Such green routes make use of biological materials such as plant-derived extracts, algae, bacteria and fungi (Gunalan et al., 2012; Teimouri et al., 2018). Hence, such materials were collectively known as biogenic nanomaterials. Among the suggested green route synthesis of metal and metal oxide nanomaterials, the usage of plant-derived extracts is a simple and easier procedure to synthesize nanomaterials at a productive scale compared to fungi or bacterial synthesis (Shanker et al., 2016).

The various parameters impacting such green synthesis by biological precursors are temperature, pressure, solvent ratio, pH of the medium, etc (Ajeel et al., 2021b). For obtaining plant-derived nanomaterials, the plant biodiversity is broadly regarded owing to the selection of prominent compounds including aldehydes, ketones, amides, flavanoids, carboxylic acids, phenols, terpenoids and ascorbic acids which are available in a huge class of plant extracts, particularly in plant leaves (Sadeghi and Gholamhoseinpoor, 2014). Many of these chemical agents can reduce the metallic salts into individual metal nano components (Srivastava, 2014). The essential attributes of such nano components have been recommended for their application in antimicrobial techniques, catalysis, biomedical therapies, optical networks, molecular labelling and identification of various biological systems (Tiwari et al., 2011).

This study was organized to investigate the present scenario of green route techniques for the synthesis of nanomaterial. Overall, it is exceptionally beneficial to track down a clean route for functionalization of metal/metal-based oxide nanomaterials along with considering the financial parts of such technique for applying in different uses. Besides, it also includes the role of various solvent agents, impacting biological extracts such as bacteria, fungi, algae and other plant-derived extracts mentioning their merits over several other solvent systems. This article provides an overview of the available mechanisms for green route techniques and their real-time applications in the global environment. In addition, the article focus on the systematic procedure for green synthesis along with its related constituents which will benevolently aid the research scientists inculcated in this branch of study. The goal of this review is to inspire more studies in the field of green synthesis of metal/metal oxide nanoparticles.

Richard Feynman is the first person to discuss the concept of nanotechnology in the year 1959 which leads to the beginning of improved nanotechnology. Later, the actual coining was made by Professor. Dr. Norio Taniguchi, Tokyo, in his statement as nanotechnology involves the extreme mechanisms of separation, aggregation and possible destruction of various components by a single atom or single molecule. In general, it deals with the science of determining matter on an atomic as well as the molecular level (Azam et al., 2012). The synthetic methods of nanomaterials and the identification of their salient physicochemical characteristics are recognized as keypoint for the drastic growth of the updated frontiers of such emerging technology (Thandapani et al., 2017). Owing to these rapid advancements, this nano field has reached sound impulsion in various branches such as waste treatment, electronics, food technology, chemical processing/design, environmental sectors, pharmaceutical industry, biomedical applications, mechanics, space design, robotics and optical networks (Ajeel et al., 2021c). The growth and consumption of nano components have greatly rendered a more stable, reliable, holistic and sustainable alternative to various technology/environment-based challenges in different thrust areas like drug delivery, solar energy storage, waste effluent treatment, catalytic conversion and even hydrogen production strategies (Mittal et al., 2013). Nanoparticles can display size-related properties fundamentally not quite the same as those of either fine particles or mass materials. A mass material has consistent actual properties paying little mind to its size, however, at the nanoscale, size-dependent properties are noticed. The fascinating and startling properties of nanoparticles are credited to the huge surface region, and this rules the commitments made by the little greater part of the materials (Rane et al., 2018). Nanoscale structures, for example, nanoparticles and nanolayers, have exceptionally high surface-to-volume also, angle proportions, making them ideal for use in different materials. There are a few strategies for making nanoparticles, including coprecipitation, inert gas condensation, aqueous combination, spark discharge, microemulsion, microwave, sol-gel, sonochemical, layout combination, and organic amalgamation. We will now momentarily investigate the strategies for the combination of nanoparticles (Aryal et al., 2019).

The research network available worldwide is busy in establishing eco-friendly mechanisms to generate components that are ecologically amiable, non-hazardous, more effective, processed through the application of green route nano synthesis and novel biotechnological routes (Bhuyan et al., 2015). Nanomaterials derived through green route procedures in a single step are eco-friendly with enormous stability along with notable characteristics and dimensional features thereby preventing the demand for pressure, enhanced temperature, pH etc., maintained during such synthesis (Li et al., 2011).

There are two unique ways by which metal/metal oxide nanoparticles can be framed. These are the “bottom-up” approach and the “top-down” approach (Pollet et al., 2011). Fig. 1 shows various mechanisms of nanoparticles employing green routes.

In this method, the selected bulk component is broken down into finer particles using the appropriate lithographic procedures such as sputtering, grinding and milling. The top-down methodology which is sourced from microelectronics has to do with a reasonable decrease or separation of frameworks in their present status by making existing advances more productive. These outcomes in a decrease in the size of the gadgets into nanoscale viewpoints (Zhang et al., 2020).

This method incorporates the nanomaterial production through the auto arrangement of atomic substances into fresh nuclei which then enlarges into a nanoparticle comprising nanoscale dimensions along with encompassing several chemicals, physical and biological techniques (Ratna, 2012). The bottom-up perspective further includes making nanomaterials and items inside the equivalent nanosphere dependent on molecules and atoms. This kind of collection happens unmistakably and sensibly, which takes into consideration an expansion in the usefulness of the construction of such materials.

Nanoparticles are components with nanoscale dimensions (1–100 nm) appearing in the region of transition betwixt separate molecules as well as their bulky counter portions (Gonawala and Mehta, 2014). Owing to their size, such nanoparticles embrace specific physicochemical characteristics inculcating a maximum specific surface distribution, enormous energy and entrapped quantum internment. They exhibit a diversified chemical environment and also can be available as metallic (copper, magnesium, gold, silver, zinc) or else constituted with metal oxide, polymers, organics, silicates or carbon (Zayed et al., 2012; Singh et al., 2017). More than its diversified chemical nature, such nanomaterials can also be generated via various geometric configurations such as cylinders, spheres, thin sheets or cyclic tubes (Ajeel et al., 2019b). Such amazing geometric patterns along with diversity in the chemical constituent of nanomaterials portray its shape appearance which is dependent on the medium type from which these nanocomponents are developed and also the quantity of selective bioactive components included in the associated medium (Tan et al., 2013). Fig. 2 shows the different shapes of nanoparticles.
Nanoparticles are of various types which include metal-based, metal oxides, alloy type, and magnetic nanoparticles.

These are collectively known as nanometals profound in nanoscale dimensions. In 1857, Faraday, Dutch Scientist, first recommended the presence of metal-based nanoparticles in aqueous form whereas, Mie recognized the quantitative nature of nanoparticles ascertained in aqueous solution. The salient features of metal-based nanocomponents include surplus surface energies, maximum surface area/volume ratio, Plasmonic excitation, drastic quantum internment and defined electronic arrangement attributed to its transition nature from molecular to metallic states.

Various classes of metal-based nanoparticles have been carefully synthesized comprising of gold, silver, aluminum, copper, magnesium, palladium, zinc and platinum. Gold nanoparticles are of much importance but lesser concern than their silver counterpart. The silver nanoparticle is widely preferred and prepared through green route mechanism due to its excellent antibacterial characteristics as well as easier reduction resulted from univalent silver to metal-based silver nanoparticles (Ajeel et al., 2019c). Moreover, it also provides the maximum performance efficiency towards plasmonic excitation (Darr et al., 2017). It covers wide applications in thrust areas such as textiles, drug delivery, medicinal activity, water treatment, bioactivity and chemical catalysis. Few other metal-based nanoparticles have been reported in several existing works of literature but counted in limited numbers.

Alloy formation by mixing several elements can enhance the affirmed range and composition of metal-based nanoparticles along with their properties. The synergistic impact of the alloying process tends to assure the increment of involved properties (Elango and Roopan, 2015). When composition and its atomic arrangement varies along with size distribution of cluster components, its embedded physical/chemical attributes can be tuned easily which has made a greater interest towards such alloy nanoparticles. Moreover, these nanoalloys can exhibit distinct characteristics differing from their counter components which lead to diverse applications in the branch of engineering, electronics as well as reaction catalysis (Flores et al., 2018).

Metal oxide-based nanoparticles can be obtained by joining two metal portions (-O-) with oxy or hydroxyl bridges (–OH–) thereby produces metal oxide or metal hydroxide-based nanoparticles in an aqueous solution.

These nanocomponents comprise two major classes of division: Magnetic nanoparticles such as Fe, Co, Ni and non-magnetic component with definite functionality. Owing to the inclusion of magnetic parts, these components can be easily subjected to magnetic manipulation (Anantharaman et al., 2018). Magnetic nanoparticles have demonstrated a valuable device in advancing the statement of therapeutic compounds; mediating the obliteration of disease cells and biofilms; and, working on the affectability of MRI techniques. The creation of particular encapsulation methodologies has shown that magnetic nanoparticles would now be able to be focused on explicit tissues, including cancer cells, uncovering the opportunities for an original customized way to deal with attractive nanoparticle-mediated treatment (Guo et al., 2018).

Nanoparticle synthesis can be performed by different techniques like physical, chemical and biological methodologies. By and large, the physical and chemical techniques are viewed as the best to get uniform-sized nanoparticles with long-term stability (Guo et al., 2018). These methodologies are costly and discharge harmful/risky materials into the climate. Poisonous synthetic substances utilized for nanoparticle union in compound techniques make the got nanoparticles less reasonable for clinical, restorative, or food applications (Huang et al., 2006). Since a few nanoparticles have been generally used in clinical items, illness determination, and beautifiers, working on the biocompatibility of nanoparticles is exceptionally significant. Biosynthetic courses of nanoparticle amalgamation are considered eco-friendly because the stabilizing and reduced agents utilized are either yeasts, bacteria and fungi and plants themselves or their dynamic segments (Augustine et al., 2014; Kuppusamy et al., 2016). Table 1 shows the merits and demerits of different methods for metal oxide nanoparticle synthesis.

The most popular technique involved in the physical mechanism for nanoparticle synthesis includes ball milling, laser ablation, evaporation/condensation, electro-spraying and pyrolysis through laser radiation. Several other tasks encompass such as arc discharge technique, atomization, annealing and metal sputtering process. Several nanoparticles comprising silver, lead sulphide, gold and fullerene can be effectively synthesized through a combined process of evaporation and condensation techniques (Fedkin, 2018; Rostami-Vartooni et al., 2016). Perhaps, such physical mechanisms are more meritorious than chemical-based approaches due to the definite impact of solvent mixing in the synthesized thin films along with the constant orientation of prepared nanoparticle distribution. Moreover, it is confirmed from the synthesis of silver nanoparticles through a compact ceramic heater supported with an inbuilt local heating element as published in the literature (Babu et al., 2015). The vapor drops to cold temperatures at a rapid rate as the temperature difference near the electrical heater is much steeper when compared to the tube furnace. This enables the generation of smaller nanoparticles in an increased concentration. Such a physical approach can be applied as a nanomaterial generator for continuous experiments involving toxicity studies and is also used as a measuring device for nanomaterial calibration equipment (Panigrahi et al., 2007).

Silver nanoparticles are synthesized from bulky metallic silver generally using the laser ablation technique (Ye et al., 2018). The ablation performance and the salient features of generated nanoparticles, in turn, depend on several factors including laser radiation wavelength impinging the metal target, contact duration of laser pulses, laser intensity, duration of ablation time and the inhibited liquid solvent with/without the influence of surfactants. A primary advantage of the laser ablation method with that of other procedures available for metallic colloid production is the definite absence of chemical additives in aqueous solution (Genuino et al., 2012). Hence, pure as well as uncontaminated colloidal particles for several applications can be made possible using this technique.

Chemical-based reactions can be processed through the addition of mechanical energy using ball milling which is much employed in organic chemistry along with inorganic solid impact mechanisms. Ball milling enhances energy efficiency and also avoids toxic solvents/reagents (Xu et al., 2019). Such reactions along with microwave assistance do not require the influence of solvents and are also processed at ambient conditions. As it is simple and eco-friendly, this ball milling technique can also be represented as a greener route in chemistry, but it is not widely recommended by researchers despite its larger potential. This process utilizes solid oxidants and other reducing agents for oxidation/reduction reactions, polymer synthesis, amino acids/peptides breakdown, coordination compounds, dehydrogenative coupling and other asymmetric organic substitutions using various catalysts. In specific, in organic chemistry-based reactions, in which organic feed-stocks are much sensitive to varying temperature levels, the mechanical-based synthesis would be much preferred. This is utilized in the present era as in the case of C–C bond formation reactions (Knoevenagel condensation, Glaser coupling, Wittig reaction, Gewald, Gringard, Michael reactions), C–N bonds formation (Preparation of imines, azines, oximes, guanidines, nitrones, thiocarbazones, amines N-arylation), C–S, C–O, C–H and C-Hal and other bonding types, cyclic reactions, oxidation/reduction pathways, functionalization of carbon nanomaterials (C60, graphene) and also in the supramolecular branch of chemistry (Renneckar, 2017).

Photochemical-based reactions under the influence of Ultra violet (UV) radiation are represented to be greener-based interactions and are also based on electronic excitation mechanisms. Recent studies incorporate the production of singlet oxygen with a prominent role observed in photo-oxygenation reaction. In this reaction substitution of molecular oxygen onto free molecules utilize the merging of various photo-chemical techniques with enzyme-based catalytic processes and usage of continuous flow reactors/micro-reactors for equipment optimization (Hrdina et al., 2012). Certain examples of such types of reactions are the production of nitrogen-based heterocyclic derivatives through photo-oxidation mechanism rendered to furan and its derivative compounds, asymmetric oxidation reactions through enzyme catalysis, manufacture of Fluorine-based organic compounds through trifluoromethylation (TFM) reaction of aromatics (Ramteke et al., 2013).

The most used chemical approaches include the micro-emulsion method, sol-gel procedure, hydrothermal technique, chemical vapor deposition, and polyol synthesis mechanism. Chemical reduction by using inorganic/organic reducing agents such as sodium borohydride is commonly involved in silver nanoparticle synthesis (Sikora et al., 2013). Several other chemical reducing agents comprising hydroquinone, gallic acid, sodium citrate, and even elemental hydrogen are used. Usually, such reactions are performed in an aqueous solution and the product obtained possesses colloidal nature (Azam et al., 2012). For this valid source of reason, the usual term recommended is co-precipitation and the same constitutes various stages in synthesis such as reduction, particle nucleation, crystal growth, coarsening, and particle agglomeration (Ajeel et al., 2021b). These prescribed techniques eventually reduce the silver ions and thereby produce metallic silver. Then the agglomerated particles get converted to oligomeric cluster molecules. Such clusters further initiate the development of metallic colloidal nanoparticles (Ren et al., 2016). The influence of certain surfactants exhibiting necessary functionalities (amines, thiols, alcohols, and acids) for creating possible interactions occluded along with particle substrates can enhance stable component growth and safeguards them from agglomeration, sedimentation, or else loss of in-built properties (Iavicoli et al., 2013). A huge quantity of efficient nanoparticles can be obtained via such a chemical reduction technique but possessing major demerits listing to the toxic nature of reagents and least generation of environmentally friendly by-products (Bano et al., 2018).

Such biological approaches involve the preparation of nanoparticles with the aid of plants, bacteria, or fungi. The basic amenities for silver nanoparticle production obtained through green routes is the silver ion solution along with a biological agent of reducing nature. In most of the scenarios, reducing agents agglomerated within the cell structures were able to act as capping/stabilizing agents by themselves rather than the requirement of the same from the external environment. Perhaps, the role of reducing actions is widely observed in the various biological systems (Gottimukkala et al., 2017). Hence, such silver nanoparticles have been effectively obtained through different biological organisms ascertaining its class to few kingdoms of biotic species such as Protista (unicellular species containing a true nucleus), monera (prokaryotic species without containing a nucleus), fungi species (eukaryotic or saprophytic or parasitic) and other Plantae (eukaryotic or autotrophic).

As the aforementioned discussions highlight that nanomaterials can be synthesized via various techniques (physical/chemical/biological), anyhow obtaining them through the first two pathways leads to toxicity issues along with certain environmental disposal problems (Tyagi et al., 2016). The physical technique needs a larger space demand and produces the maximum amount of heat generation thereby elevating atmospheric temperature output levels all around the feedstock component and besides, it is time-consuming. The prominent disadvantage in the chemical method involves the usage of hazardous chemical agents in synthesizing nanomaterials which would eventually create huge harm to the natural environment. Hence, there is a great demand arose in the entire globe for a different perspective to overcome such kind of drawbacks which established the introduction of green nano-biotechnology protocol. Such green methodology is very simple and most cost-effective along with its eco-friendly nature has attracted the most attention in recent decades (Battool and Masood, 2017). Moreover, a huge quantum of nanocomponents synthesized using such green routes has been successfully utilized in several applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biological sources for green route procedures
ce:section.ce:para: Infinite physical, as well as chemical approaches, involve energizing radiation, toxic reducing agents, and other stabilizing additives which may lead to pernicious impacts to humans as well as another biotic marine environment. Perhaps, green route procedure to obtain nanomaterials is an efficient one-pot procedure or else a single-step mechanism of biological reduction technique that surely demands relatively less amount of energy to start the biological reaction. Moreover, it is also exhibiting a cost expensive advantage (Si and Mandal., 2007). Table 2 shows that metallic nanoparticles are obtained from diverse bio-species.

Bacterial agents have been commercially utilized for several biological perspectives such as genetic engineering, bioremediation, and bioleaching techniques. The bacterial species possess the capacity to reduce various metal ions successfully and hence they are recommended as suitable candidates for the synthesis of nanomaterials (Thomas et al., 2019). For obtaining metallic and other nanocomponents, a broad class of bacterial species was much utilized. Prokaryotic agents and bacterial actinomycetes were being successfully employed for obtaining various metal-based and other metal oxide nanomaterials (Fair& Tor., 2014).

The bacterial production of nanomaterials has been followed owing to relatively easier manipulation of bacterial species (Marslin et al., 2018). Certain bacterial strains possessing definite shape/size can be mentioned here which has made an extensive application for synthesizing silver-based nanomaterials through biological reduction. This method utilizes bacterial species like Bacillus Cereus, Lactobacillus casei, Aeromanus species, Pseudomonas proteolytica, Escherichia Coli, Bacillus indicus, Bacillus cecembensis, Bacillus amyloliquefaciens, Enterobacter cloacae, Arthrobactergangotriensis, Shewanellaoneidensis and Corynbacterium sp. SH09. Similarly, several other bacterial agents such as Bacillus megaterium D01, E. coli DH5a, Shewanella alga, Bacillus subtilis 168, Plectonemaboryanum UTEX 485 and Rhodopseudomonas capsulate were being involved in gold nanomaterial synthesis (Otari et al., 2019).

Fungi-assisted biological production of metal-based and metal-oxide-based nanoparticles is another efficient procedure for synthesizing monodispersed nanomaterials with unique morphologies. They render their role as better bio-agents for the synthesis of both metal/metal oxide-based nanomaterials owing to the occlusion of various intracellular enzyme components in its morphology (Thomas et al., 2019). Competitively, the fungal species can produce maximum quantities of nanomaterials compared to bacterial synthesis. Perhaps, such fungal species possess many advantages with that of other biological agents owing to the extreme influence of proteins/enzymes/reducing agents on its cell structures (Shankar et al., 2003) The distinguished mechanism for synthesis of metal-based nanomaterials is reduction through enzymes occluded in the exterior of the cell wall or else beneath the fungal cell. Several fungal agents were used to obtain metal type and metal oxide type nanomaterials for gold, silver, zinc oxide, titanium dioxide, etc.,

Yeast components are unicellular microorganisms with eukaryotic cellular structures. A quantum of more than 1500 species of yeast components was found out. Successful production of nanomaterials through yeasts has been published by several research sectors (Diallo et al., 2015). The biological production of gold and silver nanoparticles by a metallic silver tolerant strain of yeast along with Saccharomyces cerevisiae inoculum has been already reported.

Plants possess the extraordinary potential to constitute few quantities of heavy metals beneath their specific parts. As a consequence, a biological synthesis that uses extracts from plants has reached enhanced importance as a viable, efficient, simple, cost-effective, and selective method and an excellent alternate to traditional synthetic methods for nanomaterial production. The synthesis of nanoparticles from plant sources was represented in Fig. 3 . Several plant species are utilized to reduce metallic nanomaterials efficiently via the one-pot technique of synthesis procedure (Delhi, 2016). Various researches have utilized such green synthesis procedures for obtaining metal-based and metal oxide-based nanomaterials via plant-derived extracts to facilitate its applications further. Table 3 demonstrates the synthesis of metallic nanoparticles using various plant extracts.

Plants contain biomolecules such as proteins, carbohydrates, coenzymes with enhanced potential to forego the reduction of metallic salt components into their respective nanoparticles (Malik et al., 2014). Similar to various biosynthesis techniques, silver as well gold nanomaterials were formerly evaluated through plant extract-mediated biosynthesis. Several plants Avenasativa (oat), Aloe barbadensis Miller (Aloe vera) Medicago Sativa (alfalfa), Osimum Sanctum (tulsi), Azadirachtaindica (neem), Coriandrumsativum (Coriander), Citrus lemon (lemon) and Cymbopogon flexuosus (lemon grass) and Brassica juncea (mustard) have been extensively used to prepare silver/gold nanomaterial. The vast focus of such research analysis has promptly explored the nanoparticle synthesis through an ex-vivo mechanism, whereas metal-based nanomaterial can be observed in biotic plant species (an in-vivo mechanism) successfully by the proper reduction of metallic salts (Zayabeti et al., 2018). The in-vivo mechanism of nanomaterials such as copper, zinc, cobalt, and nickel was explored in alfalfa, sunflower, and mustard. ZnO nanomaterials have been obtained through a wide variety of plant-derived leaf extracts like Crown flower, copper leaf, Coriander, China rose, aloe leaf extract, and green tea. The formation of nanoparticles using leaf extract was shown in Fig. 4 .

Solvent-based synthetic procedures form a fundamental mechanism in the globe whether it may be referred to as the green route or not. Water is considered an effective and ideal solvent for such synthesis procedures (Ajeel et al., 2019d). Sheldon's statement is referred to as “the chosen solvent is not a solvent and if a solvent is much appreciable than water, then it can be regarded as ideal”. Water is represented as the cost-effective and most easily available solvent found on earth. Owing to mass adventure in nanoscience and technology, the aid of water as the best solvent for nanomaterial synthesis has been so far carried out. For example, prepared Ag and Au nanomaterials at ambient temperature via bi-functional chemical agents such as gallic acid occluded in solution nature (Salehpour and Dub. 2014). Gold nanomaterials were obtained through a laser ablation mechanism in a solution-based mode. The oxygen accumulated in water tends to undergo selective oxidation of prepared gold nanomaterials which ultimately increased its absolute chemical reactivity, thereby inhibited a maximum impact on its structural growth (Zayed et al., 2012).

The green route technique comprises two mechanisms. Either water or natural extract may be preferred as a solvent. Both the routes have been described in the present context. The discussed contents will help the readers to obtain a better insight into various green routes, the impact of toxic and non-toxic components, and non-conventional resources procured out of natural origin. Ionic as well as supercritical liquids belong to such an emerging basis. Ionic liquids possess ions that have a sharp melting point of less than 100 °C. These liquids are recommended as ambient ionic solutions. Several metallic nanoparticles such as Ag, Au, Te, Al, Pt, Ir etc., were derived in ionic liquids. It is a simplified process of nanomaterial synthesis due to the ability of an ionic solution to act both as a reducing agent as well as a protective reagent (Bonilla and Kouznetson, 2016).

These may exhibit either hydrophobic or hydrophilic nature concerning the anions or cations involved. If a component tends to exhibit ionic tendency, it will also act as a catalyst. Ex: analog of methyl imidazolium tetrafluoroborate. Lazarus et al. prepared silver nanomaterial in an ionic-based solution. The prepared nanomaterials were present in both smaller and larger isotropic forms which can be produced using electrochemical technique too. In this method, the ionic solution was preferred as an alternate for solvent water without the influence of mechanical agitation. At first, Kim et al. synthesized a one-pot method for platinum and gold nanomaterials using thiol mediated ionic solution (Prathna et al., 2010). These mediums behave as stabilizing chemical agents to synthesize crystalline morphologies with smaller sizes. Dupont et al. recommended the usage of butyl-methyl imidazolium hexa fluoro-phosphate to obtain iron nanomaterials by reduction of Iridium (Ir). The prepared nanomaterials were of approximately 2 nm average size. Notably, the ionic solution is amicable for manufacturing biphasic forms of catalytic components with recycling ability meant for hydrogenation-based reactions (Das et al., 2010).

The merits of ionic solvents instead of other solutions comprise of the listed factors i) Ionic solutions possess thermal stability to enable their operation in a wider temperature range. Most of the solutions melt at ambient conditions and decomposes above 400 °C as they can undergo broader temperature levels (3–4 times compared to water). ii) Many organic polar compounds, gases and other metallic catalysts can dissolve easily in ionic solutions. iii) when compared to other polar alcohols/solvents, these are non-coordinating (Devatha et al., 2016). Anyhow, their polarities were similar to alcohol. iv) the miscibility characteristics can be modified by varying the nature of anions/cations occluded with them. v) Ionic solutions do not evaporate in ambient conditions like volatile solvents since they do not possess vapor pressure. vi) Ionic liquids possess dual nature because they contain both anions and cations. The drawbacks accounted for with the biodegradability of ionic solutions enable them not recommended for metallic nanomaterial synthesis. To overcome such drawbacks, many novels and benign ionic solutions were synthesized with efficient biodegradability nature.

Similarly, common solvent liquids can be transformed into supercritical liquids at pressures and temperatures beyond the critical point. In the case of supercritical liquid, the solvent characteristics such as density, viscosity, and thermal conductivity are correspondingly altered. Carbon-di-oxide forms the most available supercritical, non-toxic, and inert fluid. Perhaps, water available at a supercritical state can also act as an effective solvent system for various reactions, since water possesses a critical temperature of 646K and pressure of 22 MPa (Wu et al., 2017). Copper and silver nanomaterials can be prepared using supercritical CO2. If the solubility of metallic oxides decreases near the critical point, it may render the chance of super saturation and also lead to the development of new nanoparticles. Moreover, tungsten oxide and blue oxide of tungsten nanoparticles were synthesized using subcritical as well as supercritical methanol and water (Banumathi et al., 2016).

The environmental scattering and passive transport of decomposed nanomaterials rely on its behavior to create metastable solutions or aerosols involved in such eco fluids. The feasibility of nanomaterials in such an environment can be estimated using their intensity to aggregate with the medium of surroundings (Virkutyte and Varma, 2011). Such agglomeration forms a time-dependent event accounted with an intensity of particle mixing whereas the feasibility of involved suspension is solely evaluated by the size range of nanomaterials and its mutual attraction towards other environmental factors. The green route of silver nanoparticle synthesis from tea extract was observed to be feasible in an aqueous medium (Kalishwaralal et al., 2008).

Surface complexity impacts the intrinsic nature of nanomaterials by controlling their colloidal feasibility. The stability and nature of nanomaterials were possibly estimated via mechanistic knowledge derived from surface complexity processes. The colloidal nature of nanomaterials can be controlled by regulating the material dimension along with surface capping/functionalization mechanisms. Conversion of nanomaterials is an important phenomenon to define the estimation of their ecological influence or hazardous reactivity (Thakur and Karaak, 2014). For example, the sulfurization process provides a remarkable decrease in their toxic nature owing to the lower soluble nature of silver sulphide in the synthesis of silver nanomaterials. For various reasons, the application of biocompatible fixing agents, a greener approach of nanoparticle surface technology was developed with notable stability. Along with surface mechanism, additional key factors contributing to nanomaterial toxicity are size, structural shape, and constitution (Jurcevic et al., 2020). Toxicity studies of silver nanoparticles obtained with the aid of plant-mediated extracts showcased tremendous seed germination growth for valid activation when compared to other control treatments such as chemical treatment. Anyhow, such an increment in the growth rate parameters was not previously reported (Ahmad et al., 2010).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Operating parameters to be considered in green synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Several operating parameters such as pH, contact time, reaction temperature, pressure and method adopted tend to influence the preparation and feasibility of nanomaterials derived by biological routes. Many researchers have detailed the powerful idea of the integrated nanoparticles with various sorts of indications and suggestions by change with time and climate, etc. Certain parameters that contribute to the synthesis of biogenic nanomaterials are discussed. The pH of the reactant plays a prominent role in nanoparticle production. Variations in hydrogen ion quantities result in size differences as well as structural variation among the nanomaterials. It was noted that low pH values are preferred for larger-sized particles compared to high pH values (Shahwan et al., 2014). Large tube-shaped gold nanoparticles of size (30–90 nm) were obtained from plants of AvenaSativa at a pH of 2, whereas small-sized nanoparticles were obtained at a pH between 5.0 and 6.0. In contrast, a spherical form of silver nanoparticles was obtained with the aid of Zeylanicum bark mediated plant extract at high pH values of more than 6.0. The time required for incubation during nanoparticle synthesis tremendously influences the structure and quality of the nanoparticle. Variations in time factors impact the characteristic features of synthesized nanomaterials as that of light intensity, methods of production, and storage limitations. Longer times of nanomaterial incubation may lead to shrinkage or particle aggregation which could reduce the potential behavior of synthesized nanomaterial (Tejamaya et al., 2012). Temperature is another significant boundary that influences the union of nanoparticles utilizing all three techniques. The physical technique requires the most noteworthy temperature (>350∘C), while chemical techniques require a temperature under 350∘ C. By and large, the union of nanoparticles utilizing green innovation requires temperatures under 100∘C or encompassing temperature (Rai et al., 2006). Temperature contributes a key role in metal-based nanomaterial synthesis by influencing the particle size and structure of produced nanomaterials. If gold nanomaterials were produced with cympobogon flexosus plant extract, the less processing temperatures lead to nanotriangle formation and high processing temperatures rendered to the development of maximum count of spherical nanomaterials (Yurkov et al., 2011).

Pressure is another important parameter for the synthesis of nanoparticles. The pressing factor applied to the response medium influences the shape and size of the incorporated nanoparticles. The pace of decrease of metal ions utilizing biological agents has been observed to be a lot quicker at encompassing pressing factor conditions (Abhilash, 2012). Pressure forms a prominent factor in metal-based nanoparticle synthesis since it influences the size/shape of nanomaterials obtained. If ambient perspectives are incorporated, previous studies portrayed that the reduction process carried on metal ions with the aid of phytochemical causatives occurs at a drastic rate when compared to normal conditions. Nanomaterials are produced via different techniques which range from physical/chemical to biogenic procedures and every method among these contains both positive/negative features. As they are environmentally friendly and also non-hazardous, nanomaterials obtained through such biogenic routes are recommended to be most superior when compared with other alternative routes of synthesis. The quality and sort of nanoparticle incorporated utilizing green innovation are incredibly impacted by the period for which the response medium is incubated. Likewise, the qualities of the incorporated nanoparticles were additionally modified with time and extraordinarily affected by the storage conditions, light exposure, synthesis process (Darroudi et al., 2011).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Various mechanisms involved in green synthesis

ce:section.ce:para: These techniques are much useful for generating a highly stable and well functionalized/characterized and efficient nanoparticle compared to various chemical methods as they are not eco-friendly, have less process stability, and cannot achieve easy scale-up. Various mechanisms are available for nanoparticle synthesis with the aid of different microorganisms. The first method involves the capture of metallic ions onto the exterior surface or beneath the microorganism and after they get arrested by these metallic ions, a reduction process will take place through the enzymes. The author (Sneha et al., 2010) evaluated the phenomena of microbial substance mediated gold/silver nanomaterials through Verticillium species or algal blooms based on listed assumptions. i) Capture of Ag/Au ions on cells of the fungal surface due to the possible electrostatic attraction betwixt such ions and that of cell wall distributed with enzymes containing negative charges. ii) After that, both gold and silver metallic ions were biologically reduced to corresponding metal particles and further they undergo the growth phenomena. The two prominent factors associated with nanoparticle synthesis are NADH as well as NADH impacting nitrate reductase. Moreover, it is described that the latter enzyme was solely considered for the development of biologically mediated nanoparticles using B.Licheniformis. Nonetheless, biological routes associated with the formation of metallic salt ions and the produced nanomaterials developed by microorganisms remain undiscovered.

In the synthesis of plant-mediated extract, the metal precursor liquid is blended with the aforementioned extract at proper reaction conditions. Table 6 shows that various precursors meant for synthesizing nanomaterial from plant extracts (Table 4 ). Various parameters influencing the extract conditions include phytochemicals used, its concentration, pH, temperature, and concentration of metallic salt are strictly emphasized here which keeps control over nanoparticle growth rate, its yield as well as stability. The occluded phytochemicals observed in plant extracts have tremendous potential for reducing metallic ions in quicker periods when compared with fungi/bacteria which seek much lengthier incubation times. Hence, plant extracts are recommended as an efficient and benign feed source for both metal and metal oxide-based nanomaterial synthesis. Besides, plant extract performs the role of both reducing as well as stabilizing action during the nanomaterial process and synthesis. The various constituents agglomerated in the plant extract play a prominent role in nanomaterial synthesis, for instance, a variety of plants comprise different composition ranges of phytochemicals (Chen et al., 2014). Various phytochemicals that are included in plants are sugars, flavonoids, ketones, terpenoids, carboxylic acids, aldehydes and amides which help in the biological reducing action of several nanoparticles.

Sugars like fructose and glucose present in plant-derived extracts can be prominent for metallic nanomaterials growth. Notably, fructose can render mono-dispersive nature to silver/gold nanomaterials whereas glucose was able to showcase its importance during the growth of metal-based nanomaterials under different sizes as well as shapes (Fukushi and Sato, 2005). Flavonoids possess different functional groups which have a tremendous capacity for reducing metal ions. The active hydrogen atom was resulted owing to tautomeric conversions agglomerated in flavonoids through which enol gets converted to keto form. This conversion is confirmed by metal ion reduction to metal-based nanoparticles. In Ocimumbasilicum (sweet basil plant), enol to keto conversion plays a key role in biogenic silver nanomaterial synthesis (Mudhoo et al., 2018).

FTIR reports of green route-based nanomaterials obtained through plant-mediated extracts cited in various works of the literature concluded that nascent nanomaterials were continuously observed with protein molecules. Tan et al. experimented with nearly 20 natural amino acids to estimate their potential characteristics associated with gold metal ions reduction. Perhaps, amino acids possess the ability to reduce various metal ions. Gruen et al. highlighted that various amino acids such as cysteine, lysine, methionine, and arginine were efficient in reducing Ag ions (Imbabi et al., 2012).

Plant-mediated extracts are composed of protein molecules and carbohydrates which perform the role of reducing medium to facilitate the growth of metallic nanomaterials. Also, protein molecules accumulated with several amino functional groups can render its active involvement in metal ions reduction. Various functional constituents found in such plant-derived extracts are alkaloids, phenols, anthracenes, and flavonoids and they can produce metal-based nanomaterials. Published FTIR reports suggested that –C-O-, -C-O-C-, –CO-, –CC- functional groups act as capping ligands which is present in such nanomaterials. These capping agents stabilize the nanomaterials to reduce and agglomerate the nanomaterials. Kesharwani et al., (2009) enveloped the surface of photographic films with the aid of silver bromide emulsion. When light is incident on the photo film, silver bromide gets sensitized. If this exposed photo-film was properly placed into a tube containing hydroquinone solution, it was rapidly oxidized to quinone by the sensitized silver ion. Perhaps, silver ion got reduced to pure silver metal, which thereby remained in the embedded emulsion.

Based on photographic chemistry, it is represented that quinol or hydroquinone, or plasto-hydroquinone act as a better reducing medium for reducing silver ions to silver-based nanomaterials with the aid of a non-cyclic photophosphorylation mechanism. Hence, such experimental analysis reveals that heterocyclic components and other biomolecules accumulated in plant-derived extracts were responsible for extracellular mechanisms associated with metal-based nanomaterial synthesis (Fayyadh et al., 2020). It has been already well recommended that many numbers of plant/leaf-derived phytochemicals such as phenolic acids, terpenoids, alkaloids, sugars, proteins, and polyphenols play a prominent role in the biological reduction of metallic salt into metal-based nanomaterials (Kobayashi, 2017).

Terpenoids associated with geranium plant extract effectively showcase their prominent role in converting Ag ions into silver nanoparticles (Shankar et al., 2003). Eugenol is an active terpenoid constituent associated with Cinnamomum Zeylanisum plant extracts and it plays an effective role in reducing silver nitrate and mercuric gold chloride into their metal-based nanoparticles. FTIR spectrum demonstrated that –OH functional groups emanating from eugenol vanishes during the reaction procured during gold and silver nanomaterials formation. Once, development of Au and Ag nanoparticle takes place, other functional groups such as alkenes, carbonyl and chlorides appeared along with aldehyde and hydroxide groups (Vetchinkina et al., 2018). Nevertheless, the distinctive mechanism for the synthesis of metal-based oxide nanomaterial production through plant-mediated extracts is yet to be discovered further.

Generally, there are three important steps comprised in metal-based nanoparticle synthesis obtained from plant extracts: i) activation step, ii) growth step, and iii) termination step. In the primary step, biological reduction and nucleation of metal ions take place, in the middle step, tiny particles combine to produce larger ones spontaneously and is known as Ostwald ripening, and finally defines the structure and shape of nanoparticles in the last step (Machado et al., 2013).

Nanotubes, wires and dendrite materials can be generated by varying certain factors such as surfactants concentration, metallic precursors as well as solvent quantity. Spherical nanomaterials can be made by microwave method. Carboxyl methyl cellulose of sodium is conceived as a reducing/stabilizing and a capping agent involved in silver-based nanoparticle synthesis (Kim et al., 2016). In contrast with other heating techniques, the microwave method is advantageous to homogeneous heating methods of simple noble metal-based nanoparticle synthesis. The quickest method of nanoparticle synthesis applied for silver, gold, platinum, and palladium in aqueous solution using microwave irradiation technique at an electric power of nearly 5060 Watts has been reported with the aid of red grape medium as a capping/reducing agent (Singh, 2018). This technique constitutes various advantages when compared with conventional heating: faster processing rate, enhanced purity, reduced heat loss, maximum heat efficiency, less quantity of waste generation, inexpensive process, and minimal chance of by-products formation (Ren et al., 2016). Solvent-free operations (dry medium) of such techniques are preferred to prevent quick and unconditional solvent heating leading to drastic explosions. Certain precursors may be adsorbed onto inorganic carriers (zeolites, ceramics, montmorillite clays, SiO2, Al2O3) opalescent to microwaves and few can be contrary to exhibit strong microwave absorbance (graphite). Such supports can act as additives/catalysts (Kharissova et al., 2019). This organic carriers-assisted microwave mediated solvent-free procedures have portrayed few merits in oxidation/reduction processes, protection/de-protection, rearrangement, heterocyclic synthesis, condensation etc., contributing to several liberated products which include imines, nitro-alkenes, HCN, enamines among various products. Microwave technique can be performed in the presence of ideal absorbers; if not some other component enacting the role of heat source and thereby achieving the conversion of microwave energy into heat (Singh et al., 2018). Green mode of solid/liquid technique in microwave synthesis of inorganic constituents encompasses the feasible production of simple/double oxides chalcogenides, carbides, borides nitrides, silicides, carbonitrides, oxynitrides and components. (Example: ZrO2, Li/Co ferrites, ZnS/CuS/HgTe/CdS, MgB2/ZrB2, Sic/CaC2/WC, MoSi2/Ta2O5).

Green route production of silver and also palladium nano-spheres/nanowires/nanorods by deriving the source from vitamin B2 has been already reported. This vitamin is employed in the synthesis of nanowires/nanorods. This is a unique methodology involved in the branch of green nano-biotechnology which recommends the usage of various natural agents associated with the advancement of such branch, for instance, its impact on various tumor and cancer cells. Ascorbic acid is recommended as a reducing and capping medium along with chitosan chosen as a stabilizing agent owing to the characteristic feature associated with chitosan bonding with that of metal ions. Hence, nanoparticles involved concentration is directly proportional to chitosan concentration (Bano et al., 2018).

Photochemical mechanism-induced reactions under the influence of ultra violet radiation are concluded as greener route interactions that dominate the chemical reactivity of components involved in organic procedures. A recent review highlights the production of single oxygen and its aid in the photo-oxygenation process where molecular oxygen gets incorporated into atomic molecules, photochemical combination with enzyme-mediated catalysis, utilization of continuous flow/micro-reactors for process optimization (Pandian et al., 2015). Few examples include synthesis of heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen-assisted photo-oxidation process; enzyme-catalyzed asymmetric steps of oxidation, manufacture of F type organic components through tri-fluoro aromatic methylation supported with photocatalytic batch reactors (Karthik et al., 2013).

An aqueous solvent-based mechanism is involved in preparing compounds that induce crystallization and single crystal growth as well as poly-crystals at elevated pressures (up to 15 bar) and maximum temperature levels up to 300 °C either in water or solvent medium (Jayandran et al., 2015). The device for such hydrothermal mechanism comprises an autoclave (vertical thick lining cylinders with protective walls made of platinum, Teflon, gold, quartz, etc) containing water as a solvent and other suspended precursors. In the last decade, adjacent equipment named microwave-hydrothermal mechanism was utilized well in Chemical laboratories (Anantharaman et al., 2018). The salient feature of such a mechanism is the proximity of the development of crystalline growth, which is unstable at the desired component's melting point and however being expensive. Crystallinity and morphology control for the component's synthesis can be subjected to varying temperature, pressure, reaction time, solvent quantity, and precursor ratio. Anyhow, hydrothermal mechanism-based procedures are represented to be more inevitable for greener-based routes, being eco-friendly, and are largely applicable to synthesize enormous materials (Singh et al., 2018). This technique also minimizes the reactant loss, possibility of increased product yield and is hence useful to deliver classical and unknown nanostructures with a specific shape and operational control such as films, powders, and nano crystals.

This method is an approach that is based on aqueous solution obtained as a consequence of acoustic cavitation phenomenon formed in liquid phase neglecting the direct effect of vibrational bond energy. Chemical pathways can be intensified in such conditions in addition to free radicals and peroxide formation. The chemical reactivity, composition, and structural morphology of various materials can be possibly varied as a consequence of the collision effect (Babu et al., 2015). Fewer classes of sonochemical reaction steps can be performed in liquid phases, liquid-liquid, and solid-liquid interfaces applicable to the natural environment as in the case of sequestration of aromatic halogens. Ultrasound-assisted organic preparations and synthesis of functional materials were popular and thereby perfectly adapting to the greener chemistry branch owing to lesser energy requirement, less equipment cost (Ex: simple ultrasonic bath with 20–45 kHz frequency) along with a negligible high powered ultrasonic horn (Delhi, 2016).

Magnetic field-enabled synthesis is included as an alternative to several traditional methods in the current scenario. This may be due to the requirement of toxic chemicals or other additional procedures involved in traditional methods that consumes much energy and can deliver unwanted residue components. These additional steps can be neglected by using the application of magnetic fields during product synthesis. The magnetic field usage prohibits the development of desirable morphological structures when compared with traditional mechanisms. Moreover, it can render the possibility of inducing impacts to the final product orientation planes. Some techniques in which magnetic fields provide their assistance are solvothermal, co-precipitation and electro-spinning operations, etc (Thakur and Karak, 2014). Perhaps, the magnetic field addition can be directly implemented in an aqueous solution to influence the crystalline growth of the desired material and hence can be utilized for developing thin layers of certain composites. Such magnetic field-enabled synthesis procedures possess numerous advantages owing to their relative easiness and however occlude certain drawbacks. It may be applied to various materials whose feedstock and products containing magnetic features and the majority of literature reports employing iron oxides.

Iron oxides can adopt various morphological structures in presence of the magnetic field. For instance, it is suitable to prepare complex iron oxide nanorods through the solvothermal mechanism. This is feasible owing to the possible orientation of metal oxide crystal growth mediated through specific orientation by application of the magnetic field. If the magnetic force is involved in iron oxide synthesis via thermal decomposition, nanotubes of 250 nm are formed and such an event is not observed without the influence of the magnetic field (Iravani, 2014). Instead, the rate at which these iron oxide nanomaterials get heated through hyperthermia is eventually higher in other oxides that are obtained in presence of the magnetic field. Another study involved in magnetic influence is the chain series obtained at a possible temperature of 90 °C. Such particles possess an approximate size of about 150 nm. This could be emphasized that owing to uniaxial particle growth happens by the impact of the magnetic field. Moreover, it had a prominent effect attributed to the anisotropic mechanism of the delivering component (Laurent et al., 2008).

Various other materials are demonstrated to get influenced by a magnetic field are compounds comprising of cobalt, nickel, bismuth, and their few alloys. It is apt that the aforementioned materials possess ferromagnetic nature and are hence regarded as a limitation (Srivastava, 2014). Despite being true, the published reports available in literature incorporates these materials comprising ferromagnetic properties. Anyhow, for instance, cobalt and its nanowire carbide synthesis are deliberately known with the aid of magnetic field-mediated polyol synthesis. When compared with earlier methods, such cobalt and its carbide possessed spherical structures without the effect of the magnetic field. Perhaps, it is tangible to develop thin cobalt nanosheet films under the action of magnetic stress which thereby assists the possible reduction of cobalt-EDTA complex and hence opens a novel technique to develop thin films of nanosheets of magnetic nature occluded with anisotropic feature (Nguyen et al., 2018).

The method of solvothermal mechanism was adopted for producing bismuth nanowires utilizing magnetic stress. The usage of magnetic force has rendered a specific orientation in the case of crystal formation belonging to ferromagnetic type and in the sequence in which the delivered composite materials enabled through magnetic forces are accommodated. Moreover, this procedure relies on the methodology that is adequately being associated to be represented as a greener route of synthesis. It can be notably registered that this methodology reduces enormous waste and successfully process the synthesis, aiding to build it much more efficiently and in addition to that it does not enhance the generation of additional by-products under the action of external magnetic stress (Harjani et al., 2009).

Mostly hazardous solvent systems are involved in organic synthesis with the application of more than 10 basic rules of greener chemistry: maximum atomic utilization economy, safer and non-toxic chemical involvement without utilizing hazardous chemicals, usage of non-conventional precursors, a smaller quantity of catalyst inhibition, safer chemical solutions as well as water, or even better their negligence, easily biodegradable chemicals, minimization of energy waste, eliminating pollution effects and less chance of accidents. Solvents are considered a major pollutant source in organic treatment processes (Lazarus et al., 2012). Traditional organic chemicals obtained from oil sources are hazardous and poses bad issues to health, safety as well as environmental effects. Hence, solvents recommended for greener route techniques need to adhere less toxicity, easy biodegradability, higher boiling point, insoluble nature with water, and recycling ability. Under these operational conditions, ionic liquids, water, polyethylene glycols, and other super critical fluids are much appropriate (Kuo et al., 2017). In such aspect, zero toxic and biodegradable form of glycerol which is even the side product generated in biodiesel fabrication is most suitable for greener route-based organic synthesis and is also called organic water. The merits are extreme abundance, low cost, easier biodegradability, lowered vapor pressure as well as enhanced boiling point. Moreover, glycerol is insoluble with that of aliphatic hydrocarbons, extremely polar, able to develop Hydrogen bonds to dissolve both organic/inorganic compounds.

Among various green-based solvents, non-toxic, non-flammable, and eco-friendly CO2 (supercritical nature) is familiar from the olden days as a better solvent inculcated in polymer synthesis. Super critical water (H2O) is also available as a greener route solvent. In the current situation, supercritical fluids such as CO2/SC H2O exhibit their application in nanoparticle synthesis based on green route techniques. Such SC fluid employing methodologies have been adapted in various material synthetic procedures such as cleaning, extraction, fractionation, polymerization, drying, plating, hydrothermal steps, dyeing, biomass conversion thereby elucidating numerous benefits such as provision for solvent-free medium, simpler design, and recycling capacity, extremely enhancement in product yield, nil influence of waste water and also secondary pollution (Abdullah et al., 2018, Bussamara et al., 2013).

These ionic liquids are more suitable next to the water in greener chemistry. They are especially salts that are available in liquid form at 100 °C and even below that. Mostly, cations such as PR4+, NR4+, N-alkyl pyridinium, alkyl methyl imidazolium and anions comprising of alkyl sulphate, tosylate, fluoride, nitrate, and chloride are used. The alkyl chains in those ionic species were ethyl, hexyl, butyl, octyl and decyl. These molecules possess a significantly lower angle of symmetry when compared with classical compounds. When compared with water, in which the hydrogen atom acts betwixt several molecules, organic solvents attired to van Der Waals interaction and in ionic liquids, main forces were exerted by coulombic interactions (Chauhan et al., 2019, Das et al., 2017). These solvents are relatively stable under the thermal influence, electrical contact with fluids, extremely viscous, and preferably efficient solvents afforded with less amount of vapor pressure. The miscibility of such chemical compounds aggregated in ionic components relies on hydrogen bonding nature and polarity. Instead, these ionic solutions are employed for coal processing, gas handling, pharmaceutical production, nuclear fuel reconstructing, and also cellulose treatment steps among other uses. The catalysts in the organic process are utilized in homogeneous/heterogeneous catalysis performing either in similar (liquid media) or varying phases (solid-gas, solid-liquid, or liquid-gas) in the reactant mixture. At first, the active spots of functioning catalysts are differentiated from other components. Secondly, phase separation of catalytic product is possible, as it does not seek distillation or extraction, it is attributed to the concept of greener chemistry. Moreover, the solid catalyst of toxic nature can be replaced by using clays or zeolites. Hence, organic processes without a catalyst are recommended as the best green route which inculcates neglecting catalyst usage, reduced selectivity, the enhanced duration for synthesis, sufficient catalysts amount, hazardous nature, higher cost, insolubility, etc (Shanker et al., 2016).

The dry medium involved in the organic process is notably a maximum contribution to green chemistry involving criterions such as solvent less reagent interaction, which can either be direct including silica, alumina, clay, or zeolite (Khagnani et al., 2017). The physical techniques aforementioned such as UV irradiation, heating, microwave, or ultrasound can also be involved in such chemical reactions. The operations are much shorter with possibly limited contaminations, adequately scalable, and also economic. As cases of substituting dangerous steps along with greener techniques, it is noted that, usage of zinc and sodium carbonate despite fused metallic sodium, usage of lithium hydroxide in place of sodium hydroxide for dibenzalpropanone synthesis from acetone solution, ethanol and benzaldehyde, bromination mechanism of trans-stil benzene in ethanol in place of chlorinated solvent media, solventless synthesis of benzylic acid, etc (Devi and Singh, 2014).

Preventing accumulation of wastesavoidance of unwanted waste delivered in the organic process can be enhanced by recycling used solvents, reagents, and other catalysts; economy of the system, reduction of feed material loss and other intermediate components rendered during the final step of the synthesis, less toxic chemical reactions with the aid of little hazardous and safer chemical constituents; less toxic and safer leaching agents/solvents (water and other plant extracts), non-conventional sources of feed materials, reduction of chemical reagents to avoid unwanted dumping of waste materials (minimum reaction steps with less chemical consumption), usage of efficient catalysts and thereby achieves maximum product yield. Easily degradable products and hence non-persisting in the natural environment, contagious component prevention through real-time experimental analysis of intermediate compounds, smaller addition of feed materials to eliminate accidents. Cheaper/cost-effective greener routes and adequately contributing with better quality products and greener pathways prohibit the compliance of various environmental regulations, policies, and other laws.

Various environmental applications of such nanomaterials were depicted in Fig. 7. Different nanomaterials were involved in various applications Table 5
and Table 7. Several experimental focuses have been made to ameliorate various antimicrobial systems owing to the developing microbial resistance against the use of common antiseptic drugs and other antibiotics. Fig. 5 shows the antimicrobial activity of nanoparticles. Concerning antimicrobial experiments, metal-based nanomaterials are efficiently influenced by various microbial organisms (Anandhan et al., 2019). Such antimicrobial efficiency of metal-based nanomaterials relies on two specific factors: 1) feed material involved in nanoparticle synthesis and 2) particle size. When compared with past to present, the resistance offered by microbial action against antimicrobial application has been gradually increased and hence, it may be regarded as a possible threat affecting public health, for ex: (COVID-19 from 2020 to till now). For example, antimicrobial drug possessing resistance to bacteria contains sulfanomide resistant, methicillin/penicillin-resistant and even vancomycin-resistant features. These antibiotics meet various challenges nowadays such as fighting against multi-drug mutagenic agents and biofilms (Bharathi et al., 2018). The complete effectiveness of such antibiotics is observed to reduce rapidly owing to the drug resisting capacity of various microbes. Perhaps, if bacterial species are accounted to meet a larger dosage of antibiotics, certain diseases will anyhow persist among human beings.

Biofilms are contributing their key role in offering multidrug resistance towards heavier dosages of antibiotic substances. Drug resistance tends to occur in several infectious diseases of lung infection, bronchitis, and gingivitis. The most suitable approach for fighting or preventing microbial resistance of drugs is the usage of nanomaterials. Owing to numerous mechanisms, metal-based nanomaterials may preclude or else overwhelm the resistance to multi-drugs and also biofilm formation (Vasimalai et al., 2018). Several nanomaterials inbuilt specific mechanisms to combat against microbes (metallic nanomaterials, nanoparticles releasing Nitrogen oxide, chitosan constituting nanomaterials, etc). Nanomaterials can combat drug resistance since they perform via infinite mechanisms. Anyhow, multiple biological actions for genetic mutations are unlikely to happen within the same cell. Silver nanomaterials are ranked to be the most deserved inorganic nanomaterial and even they are well utilized as effective antifungal, antiviral, antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory agents (Mukunthan and Balaji, 2012). Concerning previous works of literature, the antimicrobial activity of silver nanomaterial can be explained as follows: i) Complete denaturation process of the external membrane, ii) development of gaps/pits in the bacterial membrane structure resulting in cell membrane fragmentation and iii) commendable interaction mechanism of silver nanomaterials with disulfide/hydro-sulfuryl enzyme groups that disrupt the metabolic structures and this ultimate step results in cell death. shape influencing antimicrobial study was also revealed. Dahoumane et al. (2016) devised the use of truncated nanomaterials in triangle form that are extremely reactive owing to the occlusion of high-density profiles on surfaces which has enhanced the activity of antimicrobial systems.

Gold nanoparticle synthesis is much useful in the extraneous advancement of efficient antimicrobial agents owing to its less toxicity, geared ability to be well functionalized, exposing polyvalent impacts as well as photo-thermal capacity (Tan et al., 2013). Fig. 6
represents the synthesis of gold nanoparticles. Anyhow, such antimicrobial activity of gold nanomaterials is not accounted for with the growth of oxygen reactive species. To determine the efficacy of antibacterial action rendered by gold nanomaterials, various researchers tried to merge nanomaterials to the bacterial cell membrane along with adequate modification of membrane capacity, which reduced the amount of ATP level. Such attachment impacted the tRNA binding ability with that of the ribosome. Azam et al., (2012) investigated the antimicrobial mechanism of copper oxide, iron oxide and zinc oxide nanomaterials against gram-negative bacterial species (Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Escherichia coli) and also against gram-positive species (Bacillus subtilis and Staphylococcus Aureus). The most extreme antimicrobial activity was observed with zinc oxide nanoparticles, whereas iron oxide nanomaterials accounted for the lowest antibacterial potentials. The observed order of antimicrobial potential of various nanomaterials was resulted to be 19.76 ± 1.41 nm for ZnO, 29.21 ± 1.51 nm for CuO and 35.14 ± 1.49 nm for Fe2O3. Such results demonstrate that nanomaterial size has played a dramatic role in establishing the antibacterial activity of each oxide sample. The well-defined mechanism of antimicrobial activity observed in ZnO nanomaterials is i) ROS development, ii) Release of zinc ion onto the membrane surface, iii) membrane malfunction, iv) entry via cell membrane, v) concentration and vi) surface area (Ramteke et al., 2013).

Previous works evaluated the antimicrobial activity of CuO nanoparticles against several microbial species which include P.aeruginosa, Klebsiella pneumonia. It was revealed that such copper oxide nanoparticles accounted to have prominent antibacterial properties against the aforementioned species (Anastas and Warner., 1998). It was identified that such nanoparticles must overcome the bacterial membrane cell wall to destruct the necessary bacterial enzymes which lead to cell death. For example, greener route-based nanomaterials showcased increased antimicrobial properties compared to other nanomaterials synthesized by chemical means or commercial methods (Thandapani et al., 2017). This phenomenon is due to that, plants involved in nanoparticle synthesis were inbuilt with medicinal properties. For instance, green route-based silver nanomaterials portrayed an efficient as well as a larger zone of inhibition towards several bacterial strains when compared with silver nanoparticles produced by using commercial methods (Singh et al., 2012).

4-Nitrophenol along with its other derivatives is accomplished to obtain insecticides, herbicides, and artificial dyestuffs which can destruct the environment as general organic wastewater pollutants. Owing to its hazardous and inhibitory effect, this 4-Nitrophenol is of big environmental concern. Hence, sequestration of these becomes a crucial task (Bhuyan et al., 2015). 4-aminophenol, a reduced compound of 4-Nitrophenol, has been produced by several fields as a side product of sulphur dyes, paracetamol, synthesis of white/black film agents, rubber antioxidants, precursors in analgesic/antipyretic drugs and corrosion retarders. The most effective means to reduce 4-Nitrophenol is to use sodium borohydride as a reducing agent along with a metallic catalyst which includes Ag, Au, Pd and CuO nanoparticles. Metal-based nanomaterials account for enormous catalytic efficiency owing to maximum surface adhering capacity and tremendous surface/volume ratio (Saif et al., 2016). Nonetheless, the feasibility of the mechanism declines as a result of substantial potential gradient betwixt acceptor and donor molecules which leads to the enhanced barrier of activation energy. Metallic nanomaterials can enhance the reaction rate by promoting the reactant's adsorption onto its surface by reducing barriers of activation energy.

Anionic and cationic dyes constitute a major classification of organic contaminants produced from diverse applications. Organic dyes render a very prominent role owing to their tremendous demand in textiles, paper mills, food, leather, plastic, pharmaceuticals, and printing industries. In textile processing, nearly 50–60% of dye media is utilized in the pigmentation process of manufacturing many quantities of fabric. After this process, about 16% of dyes were merely wasted and hence subjected to hydrosphere zone and henceforth accommodate an imperative cause of pollution owing to its recalcitrance means (Husen and Siddiqi, 2014). The contaminants arising from such manufacturing processes form notable sources of environmental contamination. They cause unwanted turbidity in potable water which hinders sunlight penetration and hence promotes the resistance to biological attacks and photochemical processes to marine and aquatic life. Hence, the proper treatment of waste effluents accumulated with dye solutions is a haunting challenge in the branch of ecological sciences (Su et al., 2014). The demand for safe and clean water is day by day getting increased.
