Obliging this noteworthy fact, usage of metal/metal oxide-based semiconductor nanomaterials for performing oxidation of hazardous contaminants has reached greater attention in material research sectors nowadays (Srivastava, 2014). In the zone of nanomaterial, semiconductor devices possess extreme photocatalytic capacity when compared with bulk materials. Semiconductor nanomaterials made of metal oxides (TiO2, ZnO, WO3, CuO and SnO2) have been preferentially involved in the photocatalytic performance of man-made dyes (Elumalai and Velmurugan, 2015). The salient advantages of such nano-based photocatalysts are processed affordable due to their higher surface area/mass ratio to ascertain the adsorptive nature of organic contaminants. The surface density of nanomaterials increases because of the availability of more active spots onto the nanomaterial surfaces (Ambika and Sundrarajan, 2015). This tends to enhance the percentage of pollutant removal at even lower concentrations. A lower amount of nanocatalyst is needed to treat the contaminated water. Similar to metal oxide nanomaterials, metallic nanoparticles also portray increased photocatalytic sequestration of several dyes. For instance, silver nanocatalyst obtained from Z. armatum plant extract was applied for the treatment/removal of various dyes.

Heavy metals (such as Cu, Fe, Ni, Zn, Cr, Co, Pb, Cd, Mn and Hg) are regarded as well-known pollutants found in soil, air, water and soil. There are infinite pathways for heavy metal-based pollution like vehicular emissions, mining waste, paper, natural gas, plastic, dye and coal industries. Certain metals (lead, cadmium, copper and mercury ions) showcases toxicity rates even at mild levels of concentration. Hence, analysis of hazardous metals in an aquatic and biological ecosystem has been a vital demand for actual remedial treatments. General techniques belonging to instrumentation systems usually deliver enhanced accuracy in multi-element detection (Singh et al., 2018). Anyhow, experimental devices to perform such detections are cost expensive, skill dependent, non-portable and also time-consuming.

Owing to its adjustable size range along with length-dependent optical characteristics of metal-based nanoparticles, they are possibly employed for heavy metal detection in contaminated water. The merits of metal-based nanoparticles as colorimetric sensors to detect the heavy metals in aqueous samples/environmental effluents comprises of cost-effectiveness, simplicity and maximum detection even at low ppm ranges. Colorimetric sensors were equipped to detect heavy metals such as chromium, mercury, cadmium, zinc and manganese present in water. The prepared silver nanomaterials with the aid of mango extract exhibited appropriate sensing for lead/mercury ions. Also, silver nanocatalysts synthesized from the extract of pepper/green tea deployed selective properties to sense zinc, lead and mercury ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges in utilizing metal and metal oxide nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Even though metal oxide nanoparticles discover broad applications in different fields, they have not many deficiencies which forestall their utilization in medicinal and other related applications. Successful stabilization is important to forestall agglomeration of nanoparticles because of the great surface energy and secure the properties of the synthesized nanoparticles. The biodistribution of the nanoparticle is influenced by cooperation with proteins through a cycle called opsonisation and these change the properties of the nanoparticles. The hydrophilic tetraethylene glycol bunch makes the nanoparticles water dissolvable and the hydrophobic carbon fasten presents security to the nanoparticle. Water solvency of the nanoparticles builds its biocompatibility. The harmful impact of nanoparticles must be considered when they are utilized for in vivo applications. Nanotoxicity is identified with (I) the conceivable arrival of (harmful) particles from metallic nanoparticles and (ii) the oxidative pressure because of the inborn attributes of the nanoparticle (morphology, surface charge, size and synthetic surface synthesis).

The green chemistry-based processes are recommended for synthesis of metal/metal oxide nanoparticles due to environmental amiability maintained through proper regulation, control measures, cleaner process and remediation technologies. This green chemistry follows certain principles which are recommended for various material synthesis. These principles are prevention of waste formation, utilize all materials into final product, less hazardous, usage of safer chemicals, solvents and auxiliaries, energy efficient, use of renewable feedstock, minimal steps, selective catalyst, degradability, pollution prevention, accidental prevention. The development of cleaner production through the greener chemistry both in the synthesis of metal/metal oxide nanoparticles by green synthesis and utilizing greener processes in the sustainability science. Practicing such sustainability ideas through greener production becomes essential to empower the economic strength and future prosperity.

‘Green’ synthesis of metal and metal oxide nanoparticles has been an exceptionally appealing examination region throughout the last decade. Huge kinds of plant-derived extracts (bio-components including plants, fungi, bacteria, yeast and other plant-mediated extracts) have been considered as effective resources accounted for the synthesis/fabrication of several materials. Among the aforementioned components, the plant extract is proved to contain maximum efficacy as reducing as well as stabilizing components for the manufacture of various materials (controlled shapes, sizes, structural morphology and other aspects). This detailed review concept was preferred to exaggerate the “state of the art” technique employed on greener route synthesis for obtaining metal/metal-based nanomaterials and its prompt usage in environmental remediation scenarios. The parameters that influence the green synthesis of metal/metal oxide nanoparticles from plant extracts, particularly plant leaf extracts and the production mechanism of nanoparticles are examined in detail. Detailed history on production mechanisms and current literature analysis on the role of effective solvents have been reviewed, exactly based on earlier literature tangible to encounter the associated issues involved in greener chemistry.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Replacement of harmful solvents in the green synthesis and process techniques, improving catalytic efficiency and selectivity afford cost effectiveness and less toxic waste disposal makes it highly recommendable for future perspective.

The primary benefits of the greener route techniques are their inexpensiveness and the antimicrobial nanoparticles combination ease utilizing nearby plant extracts without the requirement for a harmful chemical reducing agent, and extra covering specialists, as it offered bandages with high antibacterial movement.

It is mandatory to avail a deep knowledge of several microbial/biochemical constituents and various pathways occluded in its laboratory synthesis inculcating isolation and tracing of components which are exactly intruded in the reduction of several metallic salts into required nanoparticles.

In precise, future challenges and current progress associated with green perspectives for nanomaterial synthesis must be directed to extend the laboratory-based compliance to an achievable industrial mark by inculcating present/past issues in terms of health as well as environmental impacts.

Nonetheless, a greener route technique based on bio-derived materials or nanomaterials is mandatory which is to be extensively applied in the branch of environmental remediation and other broad areas like food, cosmetic and pharmaceutical industries.

Anyhow, biomaterials synthesized from marine plants and marine algae in a particular area, remain unexplored. Hence, ample chances remain possible for the discovery of novel green route strategies focussed on the biogenic mode of synthesis.
In depth scientific investigations are much required to implement the industrial production of such green nanomaterials.
The eventual disposal of such nanomaterials into ambient environments may definitely trigger unusual behavior and such concern definitely urges to be evaluated further.

Complete assessment of toxicity nature concerned with nanomaterials and effective risk management rendered by synthesis, handling of materials, its storage and disposal considerations are a serious concern.
